critique.summary: The article introduces Manyana, a project by Bram Cohen that demonstrates a new approach to version control using CRDTs (Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types). It highlights how Manyana improves conflict resolution and history management compared to traditional version control systems.
mainPoints: Manyana uses CRDTs to ensure merges never fail and always produce a consistent result regardless of merge order.
Conflict presentation in Manyana is more informative, showing what changes were made and by whom, rather than opaque conflict markers.
The system maintains a 'weave' of all lines ever added or removed, eliminating the need for common ancestor traversal in merges.
Rebasing in Manyana can preserve full history without creating fictional commit sequences, unlike traditional rebasing.
Manyana is currently a demo implemented in about 470 lines of Python, focusing on single-file operations without full version control features yet.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about CRDTs and their properties are consistent with established computer science literature on CRDTs, which guarantee eventual consistency and conflict-free merges. Bram Cohen is a credible figure in software development, known for BitTorrent, lending credibility to the technical insights. The description of Manyana as a demo project aligns with the available public information. However, the article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, limiting verification of practical usability or adoption.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential performance implications or scalability of CRDT-based version control in large projects.
There is no comparison with other emerging version control approaches or existing tools that attempt to improve merge conflicts.
User experience challenges beyond conflict presentation, such as integration with existing workflows or tooling, are not addressed.
The article does not provide independent evaluations or user feedback on Manyana's effectiveness.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the potential of CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) as a foundation for version control systems, with mixed opinions on their practicality and effectiveness compared to existing tools like Git. Several commenters highlight the challenges of merge conflicts, scalability, and the need for better conflict visualization, while others question the fundamental suitability of CRDTs for version control.

comments.author: ulrikrasmussen
comments.text: The thing about how merges are presented seems orthogonal to how to represent history. I also hate the default in git, but that is why I just use p4merge as a merge tool and get a proper 4-pane merge tool (left, right, common base, merged result) which shows everything needed to figure out why there is a conflict and how to resolve it. I don't understand why you need to switch out the VCS to fix that issue.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a practical perspective on merge tools and clarifies that the issue with merge presentation can be addressed without switching VCS, showing an informed and constructive approach.

comments.author: WCSTombs
comments.text:
For the conflicts, note that in Git you can do

    git config --global merge.conflictstyle diff3
to get something like what is shown in the article.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a concrete Git configuration tip to improve conflict visualization, directly addressing a common complaint and showing practical knowledge.

comments.author: lasgawe
comments.text: This is a really interesting and well thought out idea, especially the way it turns conflicts into something informative instead of blocking. The improved conflict display alone makes it much easier to understand what actually happened. I think using CRDTs to guarantee merges always succeed while still keeping useful history feels like a strong direction for version control. Looks like a solid concept!
comments.analysis: The comment positively evaluates the proposed system's approach to conflict handling and merge guarantees, highlighting its potential benefits in version control.

comments.author: radarsat1
comments.text:
Is it a good thing to have merges that never fail? Often a merge failure indicates a semantic conflict, not just "two changes in the same place". You want to be aware of and forced to manually deal with such cases.

I assume the proposed system addresses it somehow but I don't see it in my quick read of this.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about the semantic meaning of merge conflicts and the importance of manual resolution, questioning whether always-successful merges might hide important issues.

comments.author: BlueHotDog2
comments.text:
This is cool and i keep thinking about CRDTs as a baseline for version control, but CRDTs has some major issues, mainly the fact that most of them are strict and "magic" in the way they actually converge(like the joke: CRDTs always converge, but to what).
i didn't read if he's using some special CRDT that might solve for that, but i think that for agentic work especially this is very interesting
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced view on CRDTs, acknowledging their potential but also highlighting known convergence challenges, which is important for evaluating their suitability.

comments.author: bos
comments.text:
This is sort of a revival and elaboration of some of Bram’s ideas from Codeville, an earlier effort that dates back to the early 2000s Cambrian explosion of DVCS.

Codeville also used a weave for storage and merge, a concept that originated with SCCS (and thence into Teamware and BitKeeper).

Codeville predates the introduction of CRDTs by almost a decade, and at least on the face of it the two concepts seem like a natural fit.

It was always kind of difficult to argue that weaves produced unambiguously better merge results (and more limited conflicts) than the more heuristically driven approaches of git, Mercurial, et al, because the edit histories required to produce test cases were difficult (at least for me) to reason about.

I like that Bram hasn’t let go of the problem, and is still trying out new ideas in the space.
comments.analysis: Offers historical context and relates the current proposal to previous version control research, enriching the discussion with background knowledge.

comments.author: gnarlouse
comments.text:
I think something like this needs to be born out of analysis of gradations of scales of teams using version control systems.

- What kind of problems do 1 person, 10 person, 100 person, 1k (etc) teams really run into with managing merge conflicts?

- What do teams of 1, 10, 100, 1k, etc care the most about?

- How does the modern "agent explosion" potentially affect this?

For example, my experience working in the 1-100 regime tells me that, for the most part, the kind of merge conflict being presented here is resolved by assigning subtrees of code to specific teams. For the large part, merge conflicts don't happen, because teams coordinate (in sprints) to make orthogonal changes, and long-running stale branches are discouraged.

However, if we start to mix in agents, a 100 person team could quickly jump into a 1000 person team, esp if each person is using subagents making micro commits.

It's an interesting idea definitely, but without real-world data, it kind of feels like this is just delivering a solution without a clear problem to assign it to. Like, yes merge-conflicts are a bummer, but they happen infrequently enough that it doesn't break your heart.
comments.analysis: Emphasizes the importance of real-world data and team size considerations in designing version control solutions, advocating for problem-driven development.

comments.author: jFriedensreich
comments.text: starts with “based on the fundamentally sound approach of using CRDTs for version control”. How on earth is crdt a sound base for a version control system? This makes no sense fundamentally, you need to reach a consistent state that is what you intended not what some crdt decided and jj shows you can do that also without blocking on merges but with first level conflicts that need to be resolved. ai and language aware merge drivers are helping so much here i really wonder if the world these “replace version control” projects were made for still exists at all.
comments.analysis: Critically questions the fundamental premise of using CRDTs for version control, highlighting the need for consistent intended states rather than automatic convergence.

comments.author: socalgal2
comments.text:
> [CRDT] This means merges don’t need to find a common ancestor or traverse the DAG. Two states go in, one state comes out, and it’s always correct.

Funny, there was just a post a couple of days ago how this is false.

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47359712
comments.analysis: Points out a recent counterargument to the claim that CRDT merges are always correct, adding healthy skepticism to the discussion.

comments.author: logicprog
comments.text: This seems like an excellent idea. I'm sure a lot of us have been idly wondering why CRDTs aren't used for VCS for some time, so it's really cool to see someone take a stab at it! We really do need an improvement over git; the question is how to overcome network effects.
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm for the idea and acknowledges the novelty of applying CRDTs to version control, though somewhat speculative.

comments.author: skybrian
comments.text:
It sounds interesting but the main selling point doesn’t really reasonate:

If you haven’t resolved conflicts then it probably doesn’t compile and of course tests won’t pass, so I don’t see any point in publishing that change? Maybe the commit is useful as a temporary state locally, but that seems of limited use?

Nowadays I’d ask a coding agent to figure out how to rebase a local branch to the latest published version before sending a pull request.
comments.analysis: Questions the practical usefulness of publishing unresolved conflicts, emphasizing the importance of code correctness before sharing.

comments.author: codemog
comments.text: Nobody should have these types of problems in the age of AI agents. This kind of clean up and grunt work is perfect for AI agents. We don’t need new abstractions.
comments.analysis: Dismisses the need for new version control abstractions by over-relying on AI agents, which is an optimistic but currently unrealistic stance.

comments.author: alunchbox
comments.text:
Jujutsu honestly is the future IMO, it already does what you have outlined but solved in a different way with merges, it'll let you merge but outline you have conflicts that need to be resolved for instance.

It's been amazing watching it grow over the last few years.
comments.analysis: Recommends an existing alternative (Jujutsu) that addresses similar problems, providing a practical viewpoint on current tools.
